old_claim.note: Early Glass Lenses is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Earlier glass, crystal, and reading-stone lens traditions supplied the optical-material lineage for spectacle lenses and workshop lens grinding, without forming a one-to-one production dependency.

source_shape.source_locator: Background and early-spectacle chapters discuss medieval reading stones, corrective lenses, and the transition from lens use to spectacle manufacture and lens-grinding industries.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source traces the transition from earlier lens and reading-stone uses into spectacle lenses and later organized optical production.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports early glass and crystal lenses as historical lineage for workshop lens grinding while preserving the edge as predecessor rather than a hard production dependency.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: optical_workshops keeps glass_lenses_early as a historical predecessor with a narrowed lens-material lineage rationale.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to administrative spectacle shops with no lens-grinding or lens-material scope.
A stronger source shows early lens and reading-stone traditions were unrelated to medieval spectacle lens production.
The graph splits reading_stones and corrective_lenses into separate nodes that should replace the broad early-lenses predecessor.
short_reason: Earlier lenses provide lineage for workshop lens grinding.
